posts.title: Why this site exists
posts.excerpt: Two careers, one domain. A short note on why a working musician who builds websites finally built his own.
posts.body:
For over twenty years I've built websites for other people through [Live Web Studios](https://livewebstudios.com). Meanwhile, most weekends, I'm on a stage somewhere playing keys with [The British Invasion Years](https://britishinvasionyears.com).

Those two lives mostly stayed in separate folders. This site is where they finally share a directory.

What you'll find here over time: notes on running a live keyboard rig (Apple MainStage on an M4 Pro MacBook, a Korg Kronos and a Nord Electro 5 for regional dates), the occasional dispatch from the web side, and what I'm learning as I build AI workflows into real business processes.

The site itself is built with Astro, deployed on Netlify, version-controlled on GitHub, and built with Claude in the loop — the same stack I'd recommend to a client. Practice what you sell.

If any of that overlaps with your world — a show, a website, an AI question — reach out.

posts.title: The fly-in rig: trusting a laptop with the whole gig
posts.excerpt: How I stopped hauling boards through airports: MainStage on an M4 Pro, the libraries that carry the show, and the habits that keep a software rig honest.
posts.body:
There's a moment every keyboard player who flies to gigs knows: standing at oversized baggage watching a case roll out, wondering what's loose inside it. A few years of that and the math gets simple — the rig that fits in a backpack wins, if you can trust it.

My fly-in rig is Apple MainStage running on an M4 Pro MacBook Pro with 48GB of RAM. The heavy lifting comes from three libraries: Synthogy Ivory Upright for piano, EastWest Hollywood Opus Edition for orchestral colors, and NI Komplete 15 for everything else a sixties catalog can throw at you.

A software rig is only as good as your discipline with it, so here's the discipline:

**One concert file per show, organized by setlist.** Every song is a patch, every section that needs a different sound is a patch change. No hunting, no menu-diving under stage lights. If I have to think about the computer during a song, the rig has failed.

**Headroom is reliability.** 48GB isn't about bragging rights — it's about keeping every sampled instrument resident in memory so there's no disk streaming surprise mid-chorus. RAM is the cheapest insurance a software rig can buy.

**Rehearse the failure.** Before every run I force-quit MainStage and time the recovery: reboot, reopen, ready. Knowing it's under two minutes changes how you breathe when something flickers.

**Freeze the machine.** The gig laptop doesn't get OS updates, new plugins, or experiments during a season. Updates happen in the off weeks, then get soundchecked before they're trusted.

For regional dates within a few hours' drive of North Jersey, hardware still comes along — the Korg Kronos and Nord Electro 5 don't need a recovery plan. But when the gig is a flight away, the backpack rig has earned the seat next to me.

posts.title: Four voices, one chart: the harmony work nobody sees
posts.excerpt: The British Invasion era lives and dies on vocal harmony. A look at the charts that keep a touring tribute locked to the records.
posts.body:
Ask anyone what makes a Beatles song a Beatles song and they'll talk about the melodies. But play one with a band and you learn fast: the magic is in the stack. Those records are vocal harmony machines — thirds that split and rejoin, unison lines that bloom into triads exactly one beat before the hook.

In [The British Invasion Years](https://britishinvasionyears.com) I handle the multi-voice harmony charts — writing them, maintaining them, and updating them when arrangements shift. It's the least visible job in the show and one of the most consequential.

A few things the work has taught me:

**Transcribe the record, not the memory.** Everyone *thinks* they know the harmony to these songs. The actual voicings are frequently stranger and better than the version in your head. The chart's job is to settle arguments before they start.

**Write for the singer you have.** A chart that's correct but sits in the wrong part of someone's range is a chart that gets ignored by the second show. Voice assignments matter as much as the notes.

**Notate the breaths.** When four people phrase a line differently, it sounds like four people. When they breathe in the same places, it sounds like a record. The breath marks earn their ink.

**Charts are maintenance, not monuments.** Keys change, personnel changes, an arrangement gets a new tag for the live show. A binder of stale charts is worse than none, because people trust it.

It's arranging as quality control. The audience never sees a chart — they just hear a chorus lock into place and feel, for three minutes, like it's 1964. That feeling is the job.

posts.title: Where AI actually fits in a small business (and where it doesn't)
posts.excerpt: After twenty years of building websites for small businesses, here's my honest map of where AI earns its keep — and the three places it quietly fails.
posts.body:
I've spent two decades at [Live Web Studios](https://livewebstudios.com) watching small businesses adopt technology. The pattern never changes: a wave of hype, a rush of bad purchases, then a quiet settling into the two or three uses that actually matter. AI is following the script — so here's a map drawn from the settling, not the hype.

**Where it earns its keep:**

*The writing nobody wants to do.* Follow-up emails, service descriptions, FAQ answers, job postings. Not your brand voice on the homepage — the workaday text that's been sitting half-finished for months. AI gets a draft in front of you in seconds, and editing a draft is ten times faster than facing a blank page.

*First-pass triage.* Sorting inbound email by intent, summarizing a long thread before a call, pulling action items out of meeting notes. Anywhere a human currently skims so a decision can happen, AI skims well.

*Institutional memory.* Feed your price sheets, policies, and process docs into a well-built assistant and new staff stop interrupting your best person eleven times a day. This is the sleeper use case — it's not glamorous and the payoff is enormous.

**Where it quietly fails:**

*Anything customer-facing without a human valve.* An AI that answers your phones or your reviews unsupervised is your brand wearing a blindfold. Draft, then approve. Always.

*Math you'd bet the business on.* Quotes, payroll, margins. Language models are persuasive and confidently wrong — exactly the wrong combination for arithmetic.

*Replacing judgment you haven't written down.* If your process lives entirely in your head, there's nothing to automate yet. The documentation has to come first — and that work is valuable even if you never automate it.

The businesses getting real value aren't the ones using AI everywhere. They're the ones using it in three boring places, consistently, with a human holding the last word. If you want help finding your three places — that's the conversation I'm having these days. Reach out.
